# Buzzy MVVVP Overview

# **Buzzy MVVVP Overview**

The goal of this document is to define a lean launch version of Buzzy (web, mobile web and app) focused on hyper personalization without overbuilding. The system prioritizes automated content ingestion, structured summarization, personalization, and user-driven signals to determine what is permanently published and displayed to SEO crawlers.

The high level pieces of the MVVVP we want to have are:

| MVVVP Component | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| [Ingestion & Article Staging]() | Ingest a lot of content but only publish what people find engaging. |
| [Buzzy Browser]() | Quickly surf news personalized to your interests |
| [Personalized Home Page]() | Create a personalized stream of content |
| [User Interest Graph]() | Method by which we personalize the experience |
| [Buzzy Hyper Personalization]() | Additional details on personalization display |
| [Daily Digest]() | Way for us to keep a pulse on what’s happening and an eventual live SEO-rich daily digest. |
| [Admin Operations]() | Lean methods to manage the content in Buzzy |
| [Topic Tag Requirements]() | Full requirements for Topic Tags taxonomy and operation |
| [Email and Notifications]() | Preferences for email and push notification preferences |
| [User Feedback Form]() | Enabling users to report articles and provide app feedback |
| [Reporting]() | CSV based reports on users, comments, publishers and articles |
| [Buzzy Today Platform Glossary]() | Vocabulary and Key Terms for the platform |

# Listening and Ingestion Engine

# **Listening and Ingestion Engine**

## **1\. Ingestion & Article Processing Workflow (Python Script)**

**Purpose**

To ingest external content, filter it, normalize it, classify it, score it, and prepare it for AI summarization and candidate and possibly permanent publication across Buzzy Today.

**Workflow Steps**

*V2 02/17/26 \- Added Demand filter section (5) after categorization*

1. RSS Source Organization : Master RSS Google Sheet  
   * A master RSS Google Sheet exists for specifying RSS feeds that syncs regularly with the main database. The RSS Google Sheet will be the source of truth \- if a RSS channel is added or removed it will sync with the database.  The RSS Google Sheet has the following columns:  
     * RSS Channel Name (Text)  
     * RSS Publisher Name (Text)  
     * RSS Source Link (Link)  
     * Category Name (Optional, Text

2. Universal RSS Reader and Listener Engine:  
   * The system continuously polls RSS Source Links from the RSS Google Sheet to find new content.  
   * AI-driven RSS parser \- should be able to read any RSS feed. If an RSS feed is new the system should do the following:  
     * Strip out unnecessary content from the xml and trim its length.   
     * Pass the cleaned xml content to GPT and return the structure of the feed in structured output. It returns field names for:  
       * Title  
       * Image url  
       * Description  
       * Content  
     * Save the structure of the feed for all future use without the need for a GPT.

     

3. Knockout  
   * If an RSS feed article is too short it is rejected. There may be other knockout rules in the future.  
   * If an RSS feed article isn’t rejected it moves into the stage of Potential Candidate.

4. Image Selection  
   * First priority: Look at the underlying source article and try first image that meets our size and aspect ratio criteria (At least 400 pixels wide and 300 pixels high with an aspect ratio between 3:4 and 3:4.5).  
   * Second Priority: Select an image from Buzzy Image Bank sequentially by pull from the image bank by Category Tag  
   * Third Priority: Search Pixabay by Section and Category

5. Categorization  
   * If the RSS Google Master Sheet specifies a Category for the RSS Link, then all accepted articles will go into that Category.  
   * Otherwise, article will be categorized by Topic Tag:  
     * Each category has a set of associated topic tags. The article is checked against the topic tags of each category to determine which category it best fits in. Points for each topic tag found are assigned as follows:  
       * Title: 5 points  
       * Description: 3 points  
       * Content: 1 point

   

         

6. Demand Filter & Publishing Quotas:  
   * If a Topic Tag is followed by a user and a matching Article is found, ingest that article. Based on Topic Tag score of each article in the Categorization step (Threshold TBD).  
   * If an Article matches a Buzzword then ingest that Article.  
   * If an Article Quota still needs to be filled and an Article matches that Category then ingest that Article.  
   * 

     

       
     OR

   * Buzzword Scan:  
     * The system should compare a list of Buzzwords against the Word Scan.  
     * Buzzwords will be a topic tag unless specified otherwise in the Buzzwords config file  
     * Buzzwords contribute a Buzzword Score that increases the Candidate Score but does not affect Category Tag assignment.  
     * Testing done in apps script.

       
       OR

   * Variety:  
     * Based on a given quotient.

7. Potential Candidate Score  
   * The system assigns a Candidate Score to the article to determine if it should be considered to be shown to a user.  This score will be based on the frequency of Topic Tags and how many people follow that Topic Tag and the Buzzword Score.  
   * The top scoring articles within popular Topic Tags are selected along with some new content by a Category Tag. For example, Topic Tags that are followed by many people will be the priority articles, followed by some new content for each Category Tag as needed by looking at the Popularity Score of articles within a Category Tag.  
   * Testing done in apps script.

8. Normalization  
   * Potential Candidate articles are normalized into a unified internal format:  
     * headline  
     * full text  
     * source  
     * publish date  
     * metadata  
9. Deduplication  
   * Duplicate articles are detected and removed before summarization.  
10. Queue for Summarization  
    * Clean, categorized, scored articles are passed to the AI summarization pipeline.

**Output**

* Normalized, unique, categorized articles with precomputed Candidate Scores  
* Ready inputs for AI summarization

## **2\. AI Summarization Workflow (Apps Script)**

**Purpose**

To generate structured, consistent AI summaries for articles and editorial blocks based on section-specific configuration.

**Workflow Steps**

1. Input:  
   * The summarization engine receives:  
     * Article title  
     * article text  
     * assigned category  
     * tags  
     * image reference  
2. Configuration Resolution  
   * The system determines applicable summarization settings:  
     * system defaults  
     * category-level overrides from Summary Configurator  
3. Prompt Assembly  
   * Prompts are assembled programmatically using admin-defined parameters:  
     * lengths  
     * tone  
     * reading level  
     * hedge level  
     * emoji allowance  
   * Admins configure parameters, not freeform prompts (to preserve consistency).  
4. Content Generation  
   * The AI generates:  
     * AI headline  
     * TL;DR  
     * Key Takeaways  
     * Buzzy Take  
     * Snappy Sentence (used in thumbnail view)  
     * 2-3 Topic Tags (note these Topic Tags are not automatically added, they should be promoted based on certain rules)  
5. Post-processing  
   * Output is validated for structure and stored with the article.

**Output**

* Fully generated summaries ready for publishing

## **3\. Article Staging Database Workflow (Web App)**

**Purpose**

To control when and how summarized content becomes visible across pages.

**Workflow Steps**

1. Publishing Rules Configuration  
   * Admin configures publishing logic via Publishing Manager:  
     * Category Pulls  
     * Section Publishing  
     * What’s Buzzy Publishing  
2. Scheduled Publishing  
   * At scheduled intervals, the system:  
     * selects eligible articles  
     * ranks them using precomputed scores  
     * publishes summaries to relevant pages  
3. Page Population  
   * Content is published to:  
     * Homepage  
     * Category Tag pages

## **4\. Candidate Articles**

* As users browse Buzzy Today, they should be shown thumbnails and headlines from Candidate Articles.  
* Clicking on an article gives it 1 Permanent Article point.    
* Commenting on an article gives it 5 Permanent article points.  
* Sharing an article gives it 10 Permanent Article points.  
* Any Candidate Article with a score of 10 or higher (configurable by Category Tag) should be turned into a Permanent Article that is SEO optimized.

* F\&E Algorithm: *TODO \- Add in freshness algorithm change here (with buzzwords).*  
  * *(View points \+ Like Points \+ Comment points \+ Share points \+ Buzzword score) /  days \+ 1\.*  
  * *Calculated once (in the ingestion process) and it will never change.*

# Buzzy Browser

# **Buzzy Browser (Primary UX)**

## **1\. Purpose**

The [Buzzy Browser](https://docs.google.com/presentation/u/2/d/11eeD6Ilj-dpRK6Ob7NgU1qR08Im74tpa/edit) is the primary consumption experience for Buzzy Today.   
 Its goal is to let users quickly understand what’s happening, swipe or scroll through summaries, and generate clean behavioral signals that feed personalization, ranking, and promotion of Candidate Articles into Permanent Articles. 

The Buzzy Browser prioritizes:

* Speed  
* Clarity  
* Engagement  
* Signal generation

## **2\. Content Scope**

The Buzzy Browser surfaces:

* Candidate Articles (primary)  
* Permanent Articles (secondary)

Each item includes:

* AI Headline  
* TL;DR  
* Category Tag  
* Source  
* Optional Buzzy Take  
* Engagement controls (like, comment, share)

## **3\. Core Interaction Model**

![][image1]

Users can:

* Scroll up to see more information or tap comments to see comments, like to like the article and forward to share  
* Tap on the publisher to see the original content.  
* Swipe right to see the next story for you, or the next most Buzzy story  
* Tap on Category Tag to see jump to the next story for you or most Buzzy in Video Games, or tap on the \+ to follow Video Games.  
* Tap on Topic Tag to see the next story in the tag and follow the tag. Note: maybe these actions should just be a follow.  
* Swipe left to go back.

## **4\. Buzzy Browser Engagement and Personalization Signals**

The system tracks:

* Time on article  
* Scroll depth  
* Likes  
* View Comments  
* Create a comment  
* Shares  
* Source clicks  
* Follows

These actions can permanently publish the article. Each action contributes to:

* Article engagement scoring  
* User interest graph  
* Candidate → Permanent promotion logic

## **5\. Article Selection Logic**

Logged-Out Users

The Buzzy Browser shows:

* Top Candidate Articles by Category Tag  
* Ranked by Candidate Score and recency  
* Minimal personalization

Logged-In Users

The Buzzy Browser shows:

* Articles from followed Category Tags and Topic Tags  
* A blend of:  
  * High Candidate Score  
  * High engagement velocity  
  * Recent publication

## **6\. Candidate Article Promotion**

While browsing:

* Candidate Articles accumulate engagement points  
* Articles crossing a configurable threshold are eligible to become Permanent Articles  
* Promotion does not require immediate summarization refresh

## **7\. Output**

The Buzzy Browser generates:

* Engagement signals per article  
* Interest signals per user  
* Ranking inputs for Homepage and Publishing Manager  
* Promotion triggers for Permanent Articles

# 

# Home Page (Personalized Feed)

# **Home Page (Personalized Feed)**

The goal of the homepage is to create a highly personalized experience that can easily jump off into the Buzzy Browser or to a Section that can lead to content discovery and the Buzzy Browser.

## **1\. Home Page Approach**

* Freshness and engagement first  
* Personalized when logged in, global when logged out  
* Tag and Category driven, not section-driven  
* Composable, extensible, and bare-bones for MVP

## **2\. Home Page Model**

* A single, continuous feed (not fixed sections)  
* Built from modular content 'clumps'  
* Each clump powered by a tag, category, or signal  
* No more than one clump per Topic or Category Tag visible at a time eg avoid “tag soup”  
* Layout and order adapt per user and over time

## **3\. Home Page Sections**

* What’s Buzzy \-   
  * If logged in, what’s most buzzy in topic tags you follow  
  * If not logged in, what’s most Buzzy in Buzzy Today  
* \#Topic Tag   
  * If logged in show one of the user’s favorite Topic Tags  
  * If not logged in, show trending Topic Tag  
* \#Category Tag  
  * If logged in, show Category Tag with most Topic Tag matches for user  
  * If not logged in, show a popular Category Tag  
* Trending (what is trending in Buzzy)  
* Latest (Latest Breaking News)  
* Repeat a few \#Topic Tags and \#Category Tags based on above method and user preferences.

## **4\. Tag Ticker**

![][image2]

At the top of the Home Page is a Topic and Category Tag ticker based on the user’s interests and what is trending in Buzzy Today.  This ticker would be the old market ticker for the market’s section.

More information on the [home page](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1miUr5HTd7DcvLNANRTYlWhGj3Xy-L2frNH8V5DjZ7cs/edit?usp=sharing).

# 

# Daily Digest (What's Buzzy Today)

# **What’s Buzzy Today (Daily Digest) Internal v1**

The goal of the Daily Digest page is to provide a summary of what mattered in the last 24 hours on Buzzy Today. This should be internal at first until we can tune it for public consumption.  It will be a useful page for us to look at to see how effective it is and what happened in the platform and in the world.  The Daily Digest should be static with a URL that is based on the date, and designed to generate SEO traffic.   It’s generated once a day at a specific time and is a link in the menu (“What’s Buzzy Today”)  

This is different from the Home Page which is a live feed of what is currently “buzzing” on Buzzy Today.

The sections of the Daily Digest are:

### **1\. What’s Buzzy Today** 

(2-3 sentence narrative of the top stories) 

**Example:** Today’s Buzz was driven by tech earnings surprises, a sudden political standoff, and a cultural moment that sparked unusually heated debate in the comments.

### **2\. Top 5 Stories** 

(Headline and “why it blew up” or “why this was in the top 5” summary, thumbnail, category flair)

Example: 

**AI chip stocks surge after earnings shocked Wall Street (Headline)**

*The story exploded after traders debated whether this rally is real or another hype cycle.*

→ Read the story

### **3\. What People Are Saying**

(Buzzy differentiator, show community voice, analysis and sentiment) 

**Format:**

* 2–4 bullet insights  
* No usernames (early on)  
* No quotes needed at MVP

**Example:**

* Many readers questioned whether regulation is already priced in  
* Tech optimism is high, but trust in leadership is low  
* Several threads split sharply along generational lines

  ### **4\. By Category**

(Provide skimmability) 

**Format:**

* Category Tag name  
* 1 sentence summary (can be TL;DR of article)  
* link

**Example:**  
 **Tech** — AI layoffs sparked concern about long-term hiring trends  
 **Money** — Markets rallied, but bond traders stayed cautious  
 **Politics** — A procedural move triggered outsized backlash  
 **Culture** — A celebrity comment reignited a familiar debate

### **5\. The Buzzy Moment**

*(Lightweight but sticky top comment)*

**Purpose:** Emotional memory hook

**Input:** top comment or Buzzy Take

**Format:** 1–2 sentences

**Example:**

Today’s Buzzy moment came from an unexpectedly heated discussion from a comment buried halfway down a culture story — and it ended up with more replies than the headline itself.

### **6\. Why This Matters**

**Purpose:** Meaning \> information

**Input:** Pattern recognition across stories

**Example:**

Today’s news showed a growing gap between headline optimism and reader skepticism,  a pattern that’s been building quietly for weeks.

Once we feel comfortable sharing we can turn this on as a public feature.

# User Interest Graph

# **User Interest Graph**

## **1\. User Interest Graph Definition**

The User Interest Graph is a weighted network connecting a user to Category Tags, Topic Tags, and content, and is updated continuously by behavior.  A Interest Graph is a structured map of:

* Who a user is  
* What they care about  
* How strongly they care  
* How those interests change over time

## **2\. Core Nodes in the Buzzy User Graph**

### **A. User Node:** Represents a single user.

### **B. Category Tag Nodes:** Indicates interest in a Category e.g. Tech, Politics, Money, Markets. 

### **C. Topic Tag Nodes:** Fine-grained interests such as \#AI, \#Tesla, \#Bitcoin, \#Taylor Swift, etc.

### **D. Articles:** Articles the user interacts with that generates signals.

Temporary, but important for signal generation.

## **3\. Actions and Signals**

As a user interacts with an Article, it will update the strength of the Category Tags and Topic Tags based on the type of action.

| Action | Type | Strength |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| View article | User → Article | Low |
| Read 75% | User → Article | Medium |
| Like | User → Article | High |
| Comment | User → Article | Very High |
| Share | User → Article | Highest |
| Follow Topic Tag | User → Topic Tag | Persistent |
| Follow Category | User → Category | Persistent  |

When a user interacts with an Article, it updates the interest graph for the Category Tags and Topic Tags in that Article. 

## **4\. Example Signal Propagation** 

User likes an article in the Category Tag \#Tech and the Topic Tags \#AI, \#Nvidia, \#Chips 

User Interest Graph is updated: 

* User → AI (+X)  
* User → Nvidia (+X)  
* User → Chips (+X)  
* User → Tech (+Y)

Topic Tags should get stronger weight than Category Tags.

## **5\. Weighting Model** 

| Action | Weight |
| ----- | ----- |
| Impression | \+0 |
| Click | \+1 |
| Tap | \+2 |
| Scroll | \+3 |
| Like | \+5 |
| Comment | \+8 |
| Share | \+10 |
| Follow Topic | \+20 |
| Follow Category | \+15 |

## **6\. Time Decay**

Interests should fade unless reinforced. Topic Tag weight decays 5% per week (modifiable).  So topics like “Elections” can fade but “Tech” will stay stable.

Tags should also show up in a user’s preferences and they should be able to add or remove.

## **7\. Personalized Buzzy Browser Feed**

Rank articles by:

`Article Score`

`× User Topic Match`

`× Freshness`

If article topics overlap heavily with user graph → boost.

# Admin Operations

# **Admin Operations**

## **Sources Management** 

A table in the [Buzzy Master Admin Operations Google Sheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1RC-09q1LTMRUkhUd_abiZ8_NmW9EED-ryKIwEMkz4HA/edit?usp=sharing) listing all RSS sources connected to the system.

Table columns

* Assigned Category (optional)  
* Tag-Based Classification (Yes / No)  
* Source Name  
* RSS URL

## **Topic Tags**

A table listing Category Tags and Topic Tags in those categories.

Table columns

* Category Tag  
* Topic Tags

## **Buzzwords**

* A list of Buzzwords

## **Blacklisted Topic Tags**

* A list of not allowed Topic Tags

## **AI Summary Generation Prompts & Variables**

* Summary generation prompt  
* Summary variables by Category Tag  
  * Headline Style  
  * Tone  
  * Buzzy Take Style  
  * Reading Level  
  * Hedge Level  
  * Style Guide  
  * Emojis Allowed  
* Global Variables (TLDR length, Key Takeaways Length)

## **What’s Buzzy Today Daily Digest**

* Daily Digest Generation Prompt  
* Prompt Variables or logic for where it pulls the variables

## **Content Ingestion Rules**

* Global Rules  
* Knockout Rules

## **Reports**

* Publishing Report Card  
  * Columns:  
    * Category Tag  
    * \# of Articles Scaned  
    * \# of Candidate Articles  
    * \# of Published Articles  
    * Missing Topic Tags  
* User Report  
  * Columns:  
    * Username  
    * Email  
    * Days Member (days since registration)  
    * \# of Logins  
    * \# of Views (article views)  
    * \# of Likes (likes given)  
    * \# of Comments  
    * Favorite Section / Category / Tags (user subscriptions)  
    * Shares (number of shares made from the platform)  
    * Suggested Tags


# Definitions

# **Definitions (Old)**

**Article** — A unit of content ingested from external sources, containing the full text, metadata, and results of system processing.

**Summary** — An AI-generated condensed version of an article, composed of multiple structured elements including TLDR, Key Takeaways, Custom Headline and a “Buzzy Take”.

**TLDR** — A very short, one-to-two sentence summary designed to give users a quick understanding of the article.

**Key Takeaways** — A set of the main insights or essential points of an article, highlighted by the AI for easier comprehension.

**Custom Headline** — An AI-generated headline based on the content of the article and internal rules.

**Buzzy Take** — A proprietary AI-generated perspective or additional editorial angle presented for the article.

**Article Page \-** A HTML permalink page of an article including an SEO-optimized URL, page title, page keywords and meta tags.

**What’s Buzzy** — A summary of the most notable or talked-about articles and user comments within a given period for a category or the entire platform.

**Daily Digest** — A daily selection of the most relevant or important articles created by the system according to predefined rules.

**Section** — A major thematic part of the platform representing a broad topic area.

**Сategory** **Tag** — A leading category-related tag within a Section representing a topic, idea or place within the Section such as “Entertainment”, “Tech”, etc. 

**Topic Tag** \- A user-facing word also used to assign ingested content to a category. represent topics, themes (e.g. \#AI, \#Climate, \#Elections), places, teams or specific ideas. Topic Tags are attached to a Category Tag and the underlying articles, visible in the UI, followable by users, and used for personalization, discovery, trending, tag pages, and Homepage content grouping. The same Topic Tag system is used consistently across articles, user preferences, and feeds.

**Taxonomy** — The hierarchical structure of Sections-\>Category Tags-\>Topic Tags that define how content is organized within the system.

**SEO Tags** — System-generated tags used for search engine optimization and internal SEO logic (metadata, schema, indexing). SEO Tags are not visible to users, cannot be followed, and do not participate in Trending Tags, personalization, or UI presentation.

**Buzzwords** — Internal scoring signals used to evaluate content activity and relevance. Buzzwords are applied during ingestion and ranking to detect spikes, measure engagement, and determine what content is “buzzy.” They are not user-facing, not followable, and are used only as inputs for ranking, Trending calculations, and What’s Buzzy selection.

**Article Relevance Score** — A numerical measure of how relevant an article is to a category, computed from Topic Tag matches within the Category Tag.

**Article Popularity Score:** a number which rates how popular an article is, divided by its age for a decay factor.

**Summarization Pipeline** — The system process that generates the summary, TLDR, Key Takeaways, tags, headline, and other elements for an article.

**MyBuzzy** — A personalized feed for the user based on their category and tag subscriptions.

**Subscriptions (Categories, Tags)** — A mechanism allowing users to follow categories or tags to tailor the content in their personalized feed.

**Top Take** — The system-selected most meaningful or engaging comment on an article, determined based on interaction signals.

**Buzzy Points** — Points awarded to users for various interactions within the system (comments, likes, shares, etc.).

**Badges** — Achievements granted to users for specific milestones or patterns of engagement.

**Ranks** — User status levels based on accumulated Buzzy Points or other engagement metrics.

**Leaderboard** — A ranking of users by activity level or earned points.

**Buzzmaster** — A user who achieved the highest activity score within a category during a defined evaluation period.

**Buzzmaster Points** — Special points used to determine which user becomes the Buzzmaster in a given category.

**Classified Ad** – A short advertisement placed within the platform, with optional targeting by category or other attributes.

# Priorities and Progress

List of Priorities in Buzzy MVVVP Document is our high level priority list

1. Ingestion & Article Staging   
2. Buzzy Browser  
3. Personalization

Specific priorities

1. Ingestion & Article Staging  
   1. Fix image sizing bug  
   2. Get an image from source, then Buzzy Image Bank then Pixapay as specified in ingestion section   
   3. Activate Topic Tags  
   4. Implement Ingestion & Article Staging as per MVVVP  
   5. If there's a standard link in the article like a YouTube video include the link in our article as well along with the source link  
2. Activate \#Topic Tags on the website  
   1. Next to Category Tag on Website  
      ![][image3]  
   2. Within Article view in Buzzy Browser  
      ![][image4]  
3. We will move Date, Time and Location above the TLDR in an AP Style format  
   1. (NEW YORK) \- Jan. 20, 2026, 11:12 a.m. ET

2\. Buzzy Browser: Continue to iterate on Buzzy Browser concurrently with \#1 above 

3\. Personalization: Work on personalizing on a Topic Tag level once that's activated in \#1 above (edited) 

# Topic Tag Requirements

# **Buzzy Topic Tag Requirements Document**

## **Definition**

A **Topic Tag** is a structured metadata label that identifies the specific subject, entity, event, or concept an article is about, enabling categorization, personalization, discovery, and SEO across the Buzzy.Today platform

Topic tags have multiple functions for Buzzy Today:

* Enabling users to personalize their feeds by following topic tags  
* Organizing ingested stories into the right categories by matching topic tags  
* Prioritizing stories that have an audience eg users who follow that topic tag  
* Assisting with SEO  
* Helps organize content on the website

## **1\. Topic Tag Structure**

Topic tags are organized into Topic Tag Containers or “Buckets”

* Buckets contain Topic Tag of a specific type (eg person, place, event, etc)  
* Each tag belongs to one Bucket which belongs to one Category.  
* Categories can have multiple Buckets, but Buckets can only belong to one Category.

There can be duplicate topic tags in different Buckets eg Apple in Companies and Apple in fruit. 

All tags must have a canonical name, slug, tag type, category, and container. No freeform tag creation during ingestion but we definitely want to create a list of tags we don’t have and the recommended Bucket and database information.

## **2\. Topic Tags & the Listening Engine**

Topic tags help ingestion scoring by scanning headlines, summaries, body text, URLs, and publishers for canonical matches and synonyms.

Story score is determined by tag match weight multiplied by follower weight, recent engagement weight, and category priority weight.

Stories containing highly followed or fast-rising tags surface faster in feeds and digests.

## **3\. Topic Tags & Story Categorization**

Each tag rolls up to its parent category through its container or Bucket. Category assignment is determined by aggregated tag match scores.  
If multiple categories are matched, the highest score determines primary placement, with optional secondary placement.

## **4\. Master Google Sheet ↔ Database Sync**

The [Master Topic Tag Google Sheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1E2-zQvGfDcrfBQyYUq9iV2YBHwZAROO-Ok7UidvpYro/edit?usp=sharing) is the editorial source of truth. It contains one tab per category and one column per container with metadata rows.

The database is the runtime layer. A nightly sync validates slugs, uniqueness, and tag types before updating records.

We should have another similar sheet which syncs database engagement metrics such as followers and usage frequency for editorial visibility.

## **5\. Slugs & Permalinks**

Each topic tag should have a unique “slug” that will represent it 

Slugs must be lowercase, hyphen-separated, deterministic, and unique.  
Permalink format: /\[category-slug\]/topics/\[tag-slug\]  
Synonyms redirect to canonical slugs via 301 redirect.

## **6\. Topic Tag Database Structure**

| Field | *Type* | *Desc* |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| ID | *UUID or numeric* |  |
| Display Name | *text* | *What users see* |
| Canonical Name | *text* | *Internal unique name* |
| slug | *text* | *Unique URL safe* |
| topic\_tag\_type | *enum* | *(Company, Person, Noun, etc)* |
| Category\_ID |  | *Category it lives in* |
| Bucket\_ID |  | *Bucket it lives in* |
| created\_at | *timestamp* |  |
| related\_tags |  |  |

## **7\. Topic Tag Types**

Minimum types include: 

| Topic Tag / Bucket Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Person | Named individuals not in a specific other person related category |
| Organization | Institutions, agencies, coalitions |
| Sports Team | Sports teams |
| Brand | Consumer-facing brands and product lines |
| Product | Specific named products, models, tools, software |
| Feature | Specific feature in a technology such as Live Transition or Retina Display |
| Platform | Digital platforms, networks, exchanges |
| Law | Laws, acts, policies, compliance frameworks |
| Recurring Event | Annual/seasonal competitions, tournaments, conferences, ceremonies |
| Notable Event | Specific notable incidents, breaches, launches, revelations |
| Transaction | Trades, contracts, deals, mergers, signings |
| Concept | Ideas, strategies, methodologies, terminology, lingo |
| Technology | Technical components, standards, protocols, architectures |
| Trend | Emerging behaviours, movements, market directions |
| Metric | Rankings, records, indicators, statistics, awards |
| Risk Factor | Threats, vulnerabilities or exposure |
| Place | Physical locations, venues, circuits, event centers |
| Financial Instrument | Stocks, indices, currencies, crypto, ETFs, commodities |
| Legal & Court | Court cases, investigations, rulings, disciplinary actions |
| Media & Creative | Films, shows, books, albums, media titles |
| Academic | Notable papers, research topics and academics |
| Ailment | Disease, condition or injury |
| Treatment | Antibotic, vaccines, chemotherapy |
| Location | Town, city, state, country |
| Crypto Name | Bitcoin, Ethereum, Monero |
| Crypto Ticker | Ticker for crypto symbol such as $BTC, $ETH, $XMR |
| Fiat Currency | Names for government issued currancy such as dollars, yen, euro |
| Public Company Ticker | Ticker for public company such as AAPL, IBM, CRM |
| Company | For profit entity such as S Corp or C Corp |
| Non Profit | Non profit entity |
| Government Agency | Government funded organization such as SEC, CFTC, etc |
| Court Case | Notable court case such as Brown vs Board of Education |
| Index | Financial or data index such as DOW, Fear and Greed, etc |
| Political Figure | Elected officials past and present |
| Celebrities | Someone famous for some reason in other famous bucket |
| Athletes | Sports personas |
| Musicians | Famous musicians in pop, classical, jazz or any other genre |
| Actors | Famous actors across television, film and stage |
| Composers | Famous composers such as Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert etc |
| Scientific Term | Any term related to science |
| Conference | Any event such as G8 Summit, CES, NAB Show |
| Industry | Pharmaceutical, Oil and Gas, Hospitality |
| Protocol | Named prototcols of note |
| Song | Names of famous songs |
| Media Title | Any media title that is not a Book, Movie or Song |
| Book | Named Books |
| Movie | Named Movies past and present |
| TV Show | Notable TV Shows |
| Artistic Genre | Genre for any art such as music (Baroque, Classical), art (Surrealism) or Books (Science Fiction) |
| Award | Any award such as Emmy, Golden Globe, Lifetime Achievement |
| Historical Event | Era sch as Cold War or Event such as World War 1 |

## **8\. Plurals & Synonyms**

Each tag has one canonical singular form. Plurals and common variants are stored in the synonym table.  
Synonyms map to canonical tags and never create new tag records.

## **9\. Topic Tags & Personalization Engine**

Users may follow categories and individual topic tags. User interest scores increase via reading time, engagement actions, and explicit follows, and decay over time. Feed ranking boosts stories containing tags aligned with user interest scores.

## **10\. Suggested Topic Tag Creation During Ingestion**

* During ingestion, the system should run an AI-driven entity extraction pass after canonical tag matching.   
* High-confidence entities not matching existing canonical tags or synonyms should be added to a “Suggested Topic Tags” queue rather than auto-created.  
* Each suggestion includes extracted term, normalized slug, suggested Bucket or new bucket, proposed tag type, proposed category (or new)  
* Suggestions surface for editorial review only   
* Approved tags are written to the Master Google Sheet and synced to the database. 

# Buzzy Today Platform Glossary

# 

# **Platform Glossary**

 

Version 1.1   |   February 2026

*Authoritative terminology reference for all development teams.*

Terms in this glossary are used consistently across the MVVVP, Milestone Acceptance Criteria, and all technical specifications. When in doubt, refer to this document.

**A**

**Admin Panel / Admin Operations**

The internal web interface used by the Buzzy team to configure and manage the platform. Includes controls for RSS sources, Topic Tags, Buzzwords, blacklisted tags, AI prompt variables, publishing quotas, and reporting dashboards.

**AI Confidence Score**

A numerical value stored alongside each AI-suggested Topic Tag indicating how confident the model is in the tag's relevance to the article. Used during editorial review to prioritize which candidate tags to approve or reject.

**AP Style Dateline**

The formatted location and timestamp shown above the TLDR in each article, following AP journalism conventions. Example format: (NEW YORK) — Jan. 20, 2026, 11:12 a.m. ET

**Article**

A unit of content ingested from external sources, containing the full text, metadata, and results of system processing. An Article begins its lifecycle as a Potential Candidate and may progress to Candidate Article or Permanent Article status.

**Article Page**

An HTML permalink page of an article including an SEO-optimized URL, page title, page keywords, and meta tags. Candidate Articles have URLs but are not indexed by search engines until they reach Permanent Article status.

**Article Popularity Score**

A number which rates how popular an article is, divided by its age for a decay factor. Used for ranking articles within category and tag feeds.

**Article Relevance Score**

A numerical measure of how relevant an article is to a category, computed from Topic Tag matches within the Category Tag.

**B**

**Bookmark**

A user action that saves an article to a personal list for later reading. Bookmarks persist across sessions and are accessible from a dedicated Bookmark list view. Bookmarking is a personalization signal in the User Interest Graph.

**Buzzy Browser**

The primary content consumption experience in Buzzy Today. A swipe- and scroll-based interface that presents articles as cards, allowing users to quickly browse summaries personalized to their interests. The Buzzy Browser is the main surface for generating engagement signals that drive personalization and Candidate Article promotion. Available on mobile app, mobile web, and desktop web.

**Buzzy Image Bank**

An internal library of images organized by Category Tag, used as the second-priority image source when a suitable image cannot be found in the original source article. The third-priority fallback is a Pixabay search.

**Buzzy Points**

Points awarded to users for various interactions within the system (comments, likes, shares, etc.). Buzzy Points feed into the Ranks and Badges system. Note: This feature is defined in the MVVVP but is out of scope for the current build milestone.

**Buzzy Take**

A proprietary AI-generated perspective or additional editorial angle presented for the article. Displayed as a distinct styled element within the article card. Optional — not all articles will have a Buzzy Take. A high-quality Buzzy Take may be elevated to the Top Take position.

**Buzzword**

Internal scoring signals used to evaluate content activity and relevance. Buzzwords are applied during ingestion and ranking to detect spikes, measure engagement, and determine what content is "buzzy." They are not user-facing, not followable, and are used only as inputs for ranking, Trending calculations, and What's Buzzy selection.

**Buzzword Score**

The component of an article's Candidate Score contributed by matching Buzzwords found during ingestion. Combined with Topic Tag frequency and follower popularity in the overall scoring formula.

**Buzzword Scan**

The step in the ingestion pipeline where the system compares article content against the configured Buzzword list to compute a Buzzword Score.

**C**

**Candidate Article**

An article that has passed ingestion, knockout, tagging, and demand filtering, has been summarized by the AI pipeline, and is eligible to be shown to users in the Buzzy Browser. Candidate Articles are the primary content type displayed in the Browser. They accumulate engagement points as users interact with them, and may be promoted to Permanent Article status once they cross a configurable engagement threshold.

**Candidate Score**

A numerical score assigned to each article during ingestion to determine whether it should be surfaced to users. Calculated based on Topic Tag frequency, the number of users following those tags, and the article's Buzzword Score. Higher-scoring articles are prioritized for display; lower-scoring articles may still enter the pool to fulfill Category Tag variety quotas.

**Candidate Pool**

The set of all Candidate Articles that have passed demand filtering and are eligible for display in the Buzzy Browser and on page feeds. Visible to administrators in the Admin Panel.

**Category Tag**

A leading category-related tag within a Section representing a topic, idea, or place within the Section — such as "Entertainment" or "Tech." Category Tags sit above Topic Tags in the taxonomy hierarchy. Each article is assigned to exactly one Category Tag. Category Tags are user-facing, followable, and drive major feed sections on the Homepage. See also: Taxonomy.

**Content Clump**

A modular group of articles on the Homepage driven by a single Topic Tag, Category Tag, or behavioral signal. The Homepage is composed of multiple clumps. No more than one clump per Tag is displayed at a time to prevent tag soup.

**Custom Headline**

An AI-generated headline based on the content of the article and internal rules, including Category Tag-specific prompt variables. Part of the AI-generated Summary for each article.

**D**

**Daily Digest (What's Buzzy Today)**

A daily selection of the most relevant or important articles created by the system according to predefined rules. Generated once per day at a scheduled time. Has a date-based URL structure designed to generate SEO traffic and is separate from the live Homepage feed. Sections include: What's Buzzy Today narrative, Top 5 Stories, What People Are Saying, By Category summary, The Buzzy Moment, and Why This Matters.

**Demand Filter**

A system component in the ingestion pipeline that gates which articles enter the Candidate Pool. Uses three prioritized rules: (1) articles matching Topic Tags followed by users, (2) articles matching Buzzwords, and (3) articles filling remaining Category Tag quotas for variety. Configurable by administrators.

**Deduplication**

The process of detecting and discarding articles that have already been ingested, using a combination of URL matching and content hashing. Prevents the same story from appearing multiple times in the system or in a user's feed.

**F**

**F\&E Algorithm (Freshness & Engagement)**

The scoring formula used to rank Candidate Articles for display. Defined as: (View points \+ Like points \+ Comment points \+ Share points \+ Buzzword score) / (days since publication \+ 1). This score is calculated once during the ingestion process and does not change over time.

**Feed**

The personalized sequence of articles presented to a user in the Buzzy Browser or on the Homepage. For logged-in users, the feed is shaped by the User Interest Graph. For logged-out users, the feed is driven by global popularity and recency.

**Follow**

A user action indicating sustained interest in a Topic Tag or Category Tag. Following a tag persistently boosts the user's interest weight for that tag in the User Interest Graph and increases the probability of related articles appearing in their feed. A tag can be followed directly from the Buzzy Browser card or from a Tag Page.

**Freshness Window**

A configurable period during which the same article will not be shown to the same user again in the Buzzy Browser, regardless of other ranking signals. Prevents repeated articles in a session.

**H**

**Homepage (Personalized Feed)**

The main landing page of Buzzy Today. For logged-in users it presents a personalized, continuous feed of Content Clumps ordered by Category Tags and Topic Tags matching the user's Interest Graph. For logged-out users it shows globally trending content. Includes a Tag Ticker at the top. Adapts layout and clump order over time based on engagement.

**I**

**Ingestion**

The end-to-end pipeline that brings external content into Buzzy Today. Steps include: RSS polling, knockout filtering, image selection, categorization, demand filtering, potential candidate scoring, normalization, deduplication, AI summarization, and staging. Managed by a Python script. The RSS Google Master Sheet is the source of truth for all active feeds.

**Interest Graph**

See: User Interest Graph.

**K**

**Key Takeaways**

A set of the main insights or essential points of an article, highlighted by the AI for easier comprehension. Part of the AI-generated Summary, displayed as a structured list within the article card in the Buzzy Browser.

**Knockout Rule**

A filter applied early in the ingestion pipeline that immediately rejects an article before further processing. The primary knockout rule is minimum article length. Additional rules may be added over time. Articles that are not rejected move to Potential Candidate status.

**M**

**Master RSS Google Sheet**

The authoritative source of truth for all RSS feeds connected to the Buzzy ingestion system. Administrators add or remove feeds via this sheet, which syncs regularly with the main database. Columns include: RSS Channel Name, Publisher Name, Source Link, and optional Category assignment.

**MyBuzzy**

A personalized feed for the user based on their category and tag subscriptions. Ordered by top Category Tags and filtered by Topic Tag interest scores. Visible to logged-in users on the Homepage and in the Buzzy Browser.

**N**

**Normalization**

The step in the ingestion pipeline where a Potential Candidate article is converted into a unified internal format, standardizing fields such as headline, full text, source, publish date, and metadata, in preparation for the AI summarization pipeline.

**P**

**Permanent Article**

An article that has been promoted from Candidate Article status after accumulating sufficient engagement points from user interactions. Permanent Articles receive an SEO-optimized Article Page with a public permalink and are indexed by search engines. A Candidate Article becomes Permanent when its engagement score crosses a configurable threshold (default: 10 points). Point values: click \= 1 pt, comment \= 5 pts, share \= 10 pts.

**Permanent Article Points**

The engagement points accumulated by a Candidate Article that determine eligibility for promotion to Permanent Article status. Points are awarded per user action: View/Click \= 1 point, Comment \= 5 points, Share \= 10 points. The threshold for promotion is configurable per Category Tag.

**Personalization**

The set of systems that tailor the content feed to individual users based on their behavior and explicit preferences. Personalization is driven by the User Interest Graph and implemented across the Buzzy Browser, Homepage, and Tag Ticker.

**Potential Candidate**

An article that has passed the Knockout step in ingestion but has not yet been scored, categorized, or demand-filtered. The intermediate state between raw ingestion and Candidate Article status.

**Publisher**

The external news source or website that originally published an article. Displayed on the article card in the Buzzy Browser. Tapping the Publisher label opens the original source article.

**Publishing Manager**

The admin interface for configuring how and when content is published to Homepage sections and Category Tag pages. Controls Category Pulls, Section Publishing logic, and What's Buzzy publishing.

**Publishing Quotas**

Configurable daily limits on the number of articles published per Category Tag. Part of the Demand Filter system. Ensures content diversity and prevents any single category from dominating the feed. Quota utilization is visible in the Admin Panel.

**R**

**Ranking**

The process of ordering articles in a user's feed. In the Buzzy Browser, articles are ranked by: Article Score x User Topic Match x Freshness. For logged-out users, ranking is based on Candidate Score and recency. Ranking is updated continuously as new engagement signals arrive.

**RSS Reader / RSS Listener Engine**

The system component that continuously polls all active RSS feeds in the Master RSS Google Sheet to find new content. Includes an AI-driven universal parser capable of reading any RSS feed structure. For new feeds, the parser uses GPT to identify field mappings (title, image URL, description, content), saves that structure for future use, and processes subsequent articles without re-running the GPT step.

**S**

**Section**

A major thematic part of the platform representing a broad topic area. Sections sit at the top of the content taxonomy hierarchy, above Category Tags. Note: The current MVVVP build is tag- and category-driven, not section-driven. See also: Taxonomy.

**SEO Tags**

System-generated tags used for search engine optimization and internal SEO logic (metadata, schema, indexing). SEO Tags are not visible to users, cannot be followed, and do not participate in Trending Tags, personalization, or UI presentation. Distinct from Topic Tags and Category Tags.

**Signal**

A recorded user behavior that indicates interest or engagement and is used to update the User Interest Graph and feed ranking. Signals include: scroll depth percentage, time on article, like, comment, bookmark, share, source click, and tag follow. Each signal is stored with a user\_id, article\_id, and timestamp.

**Slug**

A URL-safe, human-readable identifier for a Topic Tag or article, derived from the tag name or headline. Slugs must be unique at the database level. Example: \#artificial-intelligence has slug: artificial-intelligence.

**Subscriptions (Categories, Tags)**

A mechanism allowing users to follow categories or tags to tailor the content in their personalized feed. Distinct from implicit behavioral signals — subscriptions represent stated, persistent preferences.

**Summary**

An AI-generated condensed version of an article, composed of multiple structured elements: TLDR, Key Takeaways, Custom Headline, and a Buzzy Take. Generated by the Summarization Pipeline.

**Summarization Pipeline**

The system process that generates the Summary, TLDR, Key Takeaways, tags, Custom Headline, and other elements for an article. Prompt behavior is configurable per Category Tag through the Admin Panel, with variables including Headline Style, Tone, Reading Level, Hedge Level, and Emoji allowance.

**T**

**Tag Page (Topic Tag Page)**

A dedicated page for a specific Topic Tag showing a description of the tag, related articles, and related tags. Accessible by tapping a tag in the Buzzy Browser or from search. Has a unique slug-based URL and supports deep linking. Following a tag from its Tag Page updates the User Interest Graph with a persistent, high-weight signal.

**Tag Soup**

A layout anti-pattern where the same Topic Tag or Category Tag appears in multiple content clumps on the Homepage simultaneously, creating a repetitive, low-quality browsing experience. The Homepage logic explicitly prevents tag soup by limiting each Tag to one visible clump at a time.

**Tag Ticker**

A horizontally scrolling bar at the top of the Homepage displaying Topic Tags and Category Tags relevant to the user's interests and current trending content. For logged-in users, tickers are personalized. For logged-out users, tickers reflect global trending tags.

**Taxonomy**

The hierarchical structure of Sections \-\> Category Tags \-\> Topic Tags that defines how content is organized within the system. All content classification, personalization, and feed logic is built on top of this taxonomy.

**Time Decay**

The process by which a user's interest weight for a given Topic Tag gradually decreases over time if not reinforced by new engagement signals. Default decay rate is 5% per week, configurable by administrators. Ensures the interest graph stays current — short-term topics like election coverage fade while durable interests like "Tech" remain stable.

**TLDR**

A very short, one-to-two sentence summary designed to give users a quick understanding of the article. Generated by the AI Summarization Pipeline. Displayed prominently on the article card, styled in the color of the article's Category Tag.

**Topic Tag**

A user-facing word also used to assign ingested content to a category. Topic Tags represent topics, themes (e.g. \#AI, \#Climate, \#Elections), places, teams, or specific ideas. Topic Tags are attached to a Category Tag and the underlying articles, visible in the UI, followable by users, and used for personalization, discovery, trending, tag pages, and Homepage content grouping. Each Topic Tag has a unique canonical slug. The same Topic Tag system is used consistently across articles, user preferences, and feeds.

**Top Take**

The system-selected most meaningful or engaging comment on an article, determined based on interaction signals. Displayed prominently within the article view. A particularly strong Buzzy Take may also be elevated to this position.

**Trending**

Content or tags experiencing a significant spike in engagement velocity relative to their baseline. Used to power the Trending section of the Homepage and to influence Tag Ticker ordering for logged-out users. Buzzwords are a primary input to Trending calculations.

**U**

**User Interest Graph**

A weighted network that connects a user to Category Tags, Topic Tags, and content, updated continuously by behavioral signals. Represents what a user cares about, how strongly, and how recently. The graph is the foundation of all personalization in Buzzy Today. Weights are increased by engagement actions (scaled by action type) and decreased over time by time decay. The interest graph directly determines feed ranking, Homepage content clump order, and tag ticker content.

**W**

**What's Buzzy**

A summary of the most notable or talked-about articles and user comments within a given period for a category or the entire platform. Used as a Homepage section and as the lead section of the Daily Digest. For logged-in users, filtered to tags they follow. For logged-out users, reflects global platform activity.

**Word Scan**

An internal representation of the significant words extracted from an article's title, description, and body content during ingestion. Used for Topic Tag matching, Buzzword scoring, and categorization. Common stop words ("the," "a," "is") are excluded. Frequent words that don't match existing Topic Tags are logged as potential future tag candidates for editorial review.

**Out-of-Scope Terms**

*The following terms are defined in the MVVVP but are not part of the current build milestone. They are included here so development teams understand the full platform context without treating these as current requirements.*

***Badges***  \[Out of Scope\]

*Achievements granted to users for reaching specific engagement milestones or behavioral patterns.*

***Buzzmaster***  \[Out of Scope\]

*The user with the highest engagement activity score within a Category Tag during a defined evaluation period.*

***Buzzmaster Points***  \[Out of Scope\]

*Points used to determine Buzzmaster status within a Category Tag.*

***Classified Ad***  \[Out of Scope\]

*A targeted short advertisement placeable within the platform, with optional targeting by category or other attributes.*

***Leaderboard***  \[Out of Scope\]

*A ranking of users by activity level or accumulated Buzzy Points, displayed within the platform.*

***Ranks***  \[Out of Scope\]

*User status levels based on accumulated Buzzy Points or other engagement metrics.*

*This glossary is a living document. Proposed additions or changes should be submitted to the product team before use in specifications or code.*

# Emails and Notifications

# **Emails and Notifications**

**Welcome Email**

When a user signs up they should receive a welcome email that warmly welcomes them to Buzzy and contains useful tips, links and an area to send in feedback.

**Email and Notification Preferences**

Users should be able to receive a daily digest of category and topic tags they are following.

Users should optionally be able to receive an email immediately when a story related to a category and/or topic tag is followed.

Users should be able to toggle between off, daily digest and immediate alerts on all category and topic tags they are following.

In addition users should be able to toggle on and off when someone replies to one of their comments.

# Buzzy Hyper Personalization

## 

# **Buzzy Hyper Personalization Display**

# ---

## **1\. Objective**

The goal of Buzzy Today’s hyper personalization system is to surface and display more relevant content to each user by combining explicit user intent (follows) with implicit behavior (signals). Personalization should power consistent experiences across the Buzzy Browser, Home Page, Section and Category views, Topic Tag views, and My Buzzy.

The system should be deterministic and extensible as signal weighting and ranking logic evolve.

---

## **2\. Category and Tag Interest Graphs**

**Interest Graphs**: As a person uses Buzzy and follows Topic Tags, they will generate a unique and highly personalized Interest Graph based on the Topic Tags they like. Initially Topic Tags will all have the same weight for a Person, but over time they may have different weights.  Also in the future we may be able to infer and suggest other Topic Tags for people based on their current Topic Tags.

**Followed Topic Tags:** Topic Tags a user has clicked on a Topic Tag to follow it.

**Suggested Topic Tags:** Topic Tags that are a bank of 10 to 20 tags stored in a user profile to suggest that they follow.  We could also show some content based on a user’s Suggested Topic Tags. Suggested Topic Tags are replaced as a user engages with Buzzy.

---

## **3\. Categories and Sections**

Categories remain a coarse organizational layer and are Parents to Topic Tags.  We will also personalize based on user activity within a Category.   Categories exist within a Section. The hierarchy is:

- Sections

  * Categories

    * Topic Tags

Certain Categories such as Breaking News may have feeds that are explicitly tied to the Category.  However most feeds should be categorized based on matching Topic Tags, meaning an ingested story will be associated with whatever Category has the most matching Topic Tags.

## **4\. Personalization Elements**

The elements that are “scored” to personalize an experience are:

	Followed Topic Tags	: A list of tags the user has explicitly followed

	Suggested Topic Tags	: A list of tags the user may implicitly have interest in

	Category Affinity Score	: A numerical score for a Category’s relevance

	Section Affinity Score	: A numerical score for a Section’s relevance

## **5\. Personalized Views** 

Ordering logic: 

1\. Show freshest (most engaged) stories with followed Topic Tags.

2\. Then a mix of

a) Newest Stories from high score or followed Categories (highest Category affinity).

b) Newest Stories containing Suggested Topic Tags

c) Newest Most commented stories

This ordering balances familiarity, relevance, and active conversation.

Notes: 

* Categories next to My Buzzy should be shown by Category Affinity, not in a specific order.  

* Stories within My Buzzy should be the color of the parent Section for the Category of the story

* Categories can be followed similar to Topic Tags

* Topic Tags need to change color when followed.

* My Buzzy is a “one and done” meaning once I see a story it won’t show it to me again unless I back up.  We will want to have the ability to bookmark Stories and also view them in my browsing history.

### **Buzzy Browser (Category)**

Display Logic:

50% Newest and most engaged Stories with followed Topic Tags

20% Newest most engaged stories within the Category 

15% Most recent stories within the category

15% Most viewed stories within the category.

First 1-2 stories should be with followed Topic Tags followed by stories randomized with above algorithm.  These are also “one and done” meaning once I see a story it won’t show it to me again unless I back up.  We will want to have the ability to bookmark Stories and also view them in my browsing history.

### **Home Page View**

Top stories in Home Page are the same as “My Buzzy”

Ordering Logic:

1. Show freshest (most engaged) stories with followed Topic Tags.  
2. Then a mix of

   a) Newest Stories from high score or followed Categories (highest Category affinity).

   b) Newest Stories containing Suggested Topic Tags

   c) Newest Most commented stories

3\. Top stories from favorite Categories.

4\. Stories containing followed Topic Tags mixed throughout.

### 

### 

### **Section Page View (Newspaper Mode)**

Ordering Logic

1\. Section Hero story: most commented story in the Section.

2\. Stories containing followed Topic Tags.

3\. Stories ranked by engagement score

### **Category Page View (Newspaper Mode)**

Behavior: \- Display the Category name to the left which can be followed. Prominently show Topic Tags the user follows within that Category followed by popular Topic Tags within the Category.

Logged-out users: Show 50% most popular Topic Tags (by follows) and 50% newest Topic Tags in the Category.

### **Topic Tag Page View**

Display all stories associated with the Topic Tag. 

Default ordering:

Engagement Score

Then most recent

# User Feedback Form

# 

# **User Feedback Form** 

**(Report / Suggest Improvement)**

This form is accessible from various parts of the platform and allows users to:

* report an article,  
* report a comment,  
* propose an improvement unrelated to taxonomy.

**Purpose**

Provide a simple and consistent way for users to report inappropriate or incorrect content, or to suggest improvements to articles or comments.

**Structure**

1\. Hidden metadata (system-generated)

Not visible to the user.  
Includes:

* report\_type: article / comment / improvement  
* target\_id: article ID or comment ID  
* page\_context (optional)

2\. Reason Selector (required)

Dropdown options vary by context.

If reporting an article:

* Incorrect or misleading information  
* Offensive content  
* Spam  
* Wrong category assignment  
* Other

If reporting a comment:

* Offensive or abusive language  
* Harassment  
* Hate speech  
* Misinformation  
* Spam  
* Other

If suggesting a general improvement:

* Improve article  
* Improve comment  
* Improve user experience  
* Other

3\. Description Field (optional)

Textarea  
Placeholder:

“Add more details (optional)”

Validation:

* Optional  
* Max 2000 characters

4\. Submit Button

Label depends on context:

* Report  
* Send Suggestion

5\. Cancel Button

Label: Cancel

Logic

* When opened, the form auto-populates with the appropriate context (article ID, comment ID, etc.).  
* User selects the reason and optionally provides additional details.  
* After validation, the report is logged in the backend and it is transferred to the Google table for the admin with the following fields:

| Column | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| **ID** | Unique feedback record identifier |
| **Submitted At** | Date and time of submission (UTC) |
| **Feedback Type** | One of: suggestion\_sectiony / suggestion\_category / suggestion\_tag / report\_article / report\_comment / improvement\_general |
| **Source Location** | Where the form was initiated: MyBuzzy, ArticlePage, Comment, Other |
| **User ID** | ID of the authenticated user |
| **Target Type** | article / comment / none |
| **Target ID** | Article ID or Comment ID; NULL for suggestions |
| **Suggestion Type** | section / category / tag (NULL for non-taxonomy feedback) |
| **Suggested Name** | Proposed name of the new taxonomy item (NULL otherwise) |
| **Reason Selected** | Selected reason for reports or general feedback type |
| **Description** | Text details provided by the user |

* No moderation workflow or review UI in MVP.  
* Success messages:  
  * For reports: “*Thank you\! We’ve received your report*.”  
  * For suggestions: “*Thank you\! Your suggestion has been sent*.”

# Reporting

# **Reporting** 

**Purpose**

Reporting provides the Buzzy Today team with structured tabular reports summarizing platform activity. Initially these reports can be auto-generated and sent via CSV / Excel.  Over time these should be part of an admin panel. These reports allow Buzzy to monitor ingestion performance, user engagement, article performance, comment quality, and the generation of What’s Buzzy summaries. The emphasis is on *exportable tabular data*, not visual dashboards. Reports serve operational and editorial decision-making, helping the team choose top-performing stories, evaluate source reliability, and understand user behavior.

**Workflow**

Users emails are added to a chron job to receive reports.

Or this can be setup as an admin page whereby an administrator navigates to a Reporting section to access Reports.

Reports should be yesterday, last week, all time.

## **Publisher Report (Sources Performance)**

**Purpose**

To evaluate how each publisher (RSS source) contributes to Buzzy, ensuring feeds are active and identifying sources that generate valuable content.

Table Columns

* Publisher Name  
* RSS Feed or Source URL  
* Category  
* Date Last Pull  
* Total Articles Pulled  
* Total Articles Generated (summaries created)  
* \# of Clicks  
* \# of Comments  
* \# of Likes  
* \# of Shares

How we use this data

* Ensuring feeds don’t go dark  
* Understanding how many articles we generate vs. reject  
* Identifying which sources engage users most

## **User Report**

**Purpose**

To analyze user behavior and identify high-engagement and high-value users.

Table Columns

*(Matches Users Management table)*

* Username  
* Location if shared  
* Email  
* Days Member  
* \# of Logins  
* \# of Views  
* \# of Likes  
* \# of Comments  
* Favorite Categories  
* Followed Topic Tags  
* Unfollowed Topic Tags  
* Shares  
* Suggested Tags (optional or AI-derived)

## **Comment Report**

**Purpose**

To surface high-quality comments, moderate engagement, and support editorial workflows (e.g., highlighting comments for summaries).

Table Columns

* Comment Name  
* Username  
* Section  
* Category  
* Article Name  
* Likes  
* Replies  
* Shares

## **Article Report**

**Purpose**

To assess article performance and support editorial choices.

Table Columns

* Article Name  
* Section  
* Category  
* Comments  
* Likes  
* Views  
* Shares  
* Permalink

**Logic**

1\. Reports reflect logged events. Data originates from the Logging & Reporting system that tracks:

* user actions (views, comments, likes, shares)  
* ingestion events  
* summarization events  
* publication events

2\. Read-only data. Admins cannot edit rows. Reports are informational.

3\. No dashboards or graphs. 

4\. Data is stored for manual export to external analytics systems. Automated integration is not required.
